policy_statement: Implement procurement policy mechanisms that advantage bids/projects using materials that meet or exceed GWP targets.
mechanism_description: The state implements procurement advantages for bids using products that meet or exceed GWP targets, which encourages lower-GWP material choices and reduces embodied emissions from construction and purchased goods.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement rules → material substitution to lower-GWP products → ↓ embodied (lifecycle) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Public-sector (and sometimes publicly funded project) procurement and bid evaluation is modified to favor or set products meeting specified GWP/embodied-carbon targets (often via EPD-based benchmarks).  
(2) Mediating physical/process mechanism: Manufacturers and suppliers respond by reducing emissions intensity (fuel switching, process efficiency, clinker substitution, recycled content, low-carbon electricity, improved logistics) and buyers substitute toward lower-GWP materials/designs.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower upstream and manufacturing-related CO₂e per unit of material leads to reduced embodied emissions for buildings/infrastructure and other procured goods, yielding net GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle (embodied) GHG emissions from construction and purchased goods by shifting procurement to lower-GWP materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: procurement preference → lower-GWP material selection and production → reduced embodied/lifecycle GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: the primary, intended, and largest climate outcome is CO₂e reduction in industrial/material supply chains; any co-benefits (cost, waste reduction, innovation) are secondary.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a policy also reduces material quantity (design optimization) that could resemble resource efficiency, but this mechanism is explicitly about lower-GWP choices/standards; if offsets/biogenic accounting are allowed, mitigation remains the main category though quality varies.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using procurement advantages tied to GWP targets works through the same embodied-emissions reduction pathway, making Mitigation the correct primary category across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: specific instruments/material scopes (e.g., cement vs. steel), accounting boundaries (cradle-to-gate vs. whole-life), and enforcement strength require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could partially overlap with Resource Efficiency if procurement advantages are paired with explicit material-reduction/optimization requirements; could be undermined if GWP targets allow low-integrity offsets or inconsistent EPD system boundaries, but the dominant intent/effect remains mitigation via embodied-emissions reductions.
key_indicators: "implement policy mechanisms"; "provide advantages to projects or procurement bids"; "utilizing products that meet or exceed GWP targets"
co_benefits: Potential air-quality/health benefits from cleaner industrial processes; potential waste reduction from increased recycled content
instance_edge_case_notes: Although phrased as “provide advantages” (preference) rather than a hard limit, it still functions as a procurement requirement/criterion (e.g., bid scoring preference) rather than an incentive program.
